content:
# Acts Chapter 14 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 242-243 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_014_page_001.jpg, ACT_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it happened in Iconium, according-to the same [pattern], they entered into the synagogue of the Jews and spoke in-such-a-way-that a great multitude both of Jews and of Greeks believed.

**2** But the disobedient Jews stirred-up and embittered the souls of the gentiles against the brothers.

**3** Therefore indeed a considerable time they stayed, speaking-boldly upon the Lord, the [one] bearing-witness to the word of his grace, granting signs and wonders to happen through their hands.

**4** And the multitude of the city was divided, and some indeed were with the Jews, but some [were] with the apostles.

**5** And when there was an impulse of both the gentiles and Jews with their rulers, to mistreat and to stone them,

**6** having become-aware-of [it], they fled-down to the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra and Derbe, and the surrounding-region,

**7** and there they were evangelizing.

**8** And a certain man in Lystra, powerless [in his] feet, was sitting, lame from [the] womb of his mother, who never had walked.

**9** This [one] was hearing Paul speaking, who, having gazed-intently at him and having seen that he has faith to be saved,

**10** said with a loud voice, "Stand upright upon your feet!" And he leaped-up and was walking.

**11** And the crowds, having seen what Paul did, lifted-up their voice in-Lycaonian, saying, "The gods, having been made-like men, came-down to us!"

**12** And they were calling Barnabas, Zeus, and Paul, Hermes, since he was the [one] leading the word.

**13** And the priest of Zeus, the [one] being before the city, having brought bulls and garlands to the gates, was wanting together-with the crowds to offer-sacrifice.

**14** But having heard, the apostles Barnabas and Paul, having torn their garments, rushed-out into the crowd, crying-out

**15** and saying, "Men, why [are] you doing these-things? We also are men of-like-nature with you, evangelizing you to turn from these vain-things to [the] living God, who made the heaven and the earth and the sea and all the [things] in them;

**16** who in the generations having passed-by allowed all the nations to go [in] their [own] ways;

**17** and-yet he did not leave himself without-witness, doing-good, giving you rains from heaven and fruitful seasons, filling your hearts [with] food and gladness."

**18** And saying these-things, with-difficulty they restrained the crowds [from] the [intent] not to sacrifice to them.

**19** But Jews came-upon [them] from Antioch and Iconium, and having persuaded the crowds and having stoned Paul, they dragged [him] outside the city, supposing him to have died.

**20** But [when] the disciples surrounded him, having risen-up he entered into the city. And on the next-day he went-out with Barnabas to Derbe.

**21** And having evangelized that city and having made-disciples [of] a considerable [number], they returned to Lystra and to Iconium and to Antioch,

**22** strengthening the souls of the disciples, exhorting [them] to remain in the faith, and that through many tribulations it-is-necessary [for] us to enter into the kingdom of God.

**23** And having appointed elders for them in each church, having prayed with fastings, they committed them to the Lord in whom they had believed.

**24** And having passed-through Pisidia, they came to Pamphylia,

**25** and having spoken the word in Perga, they went-down to Attalia,

**26** and from-there they sailed-away to Antioch, from-where they had been delivered-over to the grace of God for the work which they fulfilled.

**27** And having arrived and having gathered-together the church, they were reporting-back as-many-things-as God did with them, and that he opened to the nations a door of faith.

**28** And they were spending not a little time with the disciples.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Verse 2:** Sinaiticus reads "disobedient" (apeithesantes) Jews rather than "unbelieving" (apeithouontes) as found in some manuscripts. The aorist participle in Sinaiticus suggests a decisive act of disobedience rather than an ongoing state.

**Verse 3:** Sinaiticus connects the Lord as the one "bearing-witness to the word of his grace" -- the participle martyrounti agrees with kyrio (Lord), making Christ the active agent of the signs.

**Verse 6:** Sinaiticus reads "fled-down" (katephygon) to the cities of Lycaonia, consistent with the geographical descent from the Phrygian highlands.

**Verse 8:** Sinaiticus reads "to be saved" (tou sothenai) where later manuscripts read "to be healed" (tou iathenai). This is a significant Sinaiticus variant -- the lame man's faith was directed toward salvation broadly, not merely physical healing.

**Verse 9:** The text reads "has faith" (echei pistin) in the present tense, indicating Paul perceived an existing, active faith in the man.

**Verse 11:** Sinaiticus preserves "in-Lycaonian" (Lykaonisti), the local dialect of Lycaonia, explaining why Paul and Barnabas did not immediately understand what the crowds were planning.

**Verse 12:** The identification of Barnabas as Zeus and Paul as Hermes reflects the local Lycaonian tradition of divine visitation (cf. the myth of Philemon and Baucis visited by Zeus and Hermes in this same region, preserved by Ovid).

**Verse 19:** Sinaiticus reads "Jews came-upon" (epelthan Ioudaioi) with the hostile sense of the verb. The stoning of Paul at Lystra represents a dramatic reversal -- from being worshipped as a god to being left for dead.

**Verse 23:** Sinaiticus uses cheirotonesantes (having appointed by stretching out the hand), indicating formal appointment of elders (presbyterous) in each congregation -- an important ecclesiological detail.

**Verse 26:** Sinaiticus reads "delivered-over" (paradedomenoi) to the grace of God, using the same verb-root (paradidomi) elsewhere used for being "handed over" to trial -- here inverted to express divine commissioning.

**Verse 28:** Sinaiticus reads "not a little" (ouk oligon) time -- a characteristic Lukan litotes meaning "a considerable/great [amount of]" time.

-- EMET
